slides.title: Air-Conditioning
bullets: Refrigeration process applied to comfort cooling
Classified as a high-temperature refrigeration system
Uses the same four components: compressor, condenser, metering device, evaporator
Absorbs heat indoors and puts it outside

slides.title: Structural Heat Gain
bullets: Heat leaks into a structure by conduction, infiltration, and radiation
Summer solar load is higher on the east and west sides
Attic ventilation can help relieve solar load on the ceiling
Accomplished with powered or natural-draft ventilation systems

slides.title: Structural Heat Gain Conduction and Infiltration
bullets: Heat enters through walls, closed windows, and closed doors by conduction
Infiltration through cracks around doors and windows
Infiltrating air has different humidity characteristics depending on location

slides.title: Structural Heat Gain Attic Ventilation
bullets: A ventilated attic helps keep solar heat from the ceiling of the house
Figure 36.2

slides.title: Structural Heat Gain Different Locations
bullets: Figure 36.4 shows inside/outside temperature differences for different climates

slides.title: Evaporative Cooling
bullets: Used primarily in climates with low humidity
Uses fiber mounted in a frame with water running over the fiber as cooling media
Fresh air is drawn through the fiber and cooled by evaporation
Must provide an outlet for the air to leave the structure
Three common configurations: drip-type, spray or singer-type, rotary-type

slides.title: An Evaporative Cooler
bullets: Figure 36.5 An evaporative cooler

slides.title: Package-Type and Split-Type Air-Conditioning Systems
bullets: Air-conditioning systems use the same four main components
Package units are self-contained with all components in one cabinet
Split systems have condenser outside connected to evaporator by refrigerant lines
Ductless split systems are very popular

slides.title: The Evaporator
bullets: Transfers heat from air to refrigerant
Absorbing sensible heat lowers air temperature
Absorbing latent heat removes moisture as condensate
Typical room conditions: 75°F dry-bulb, 50% RH, 62.5°F wet-bulb
Evaporator coil operates at about 40°F

slides.title: The Evaporator Humidity and Airflow
bullets: Air leaves the coil at about 55°F dry-bulb and about 95% humidity
High humidity results from cooling; relative humidity falls when mixed
In humid climates coil temperature may be lower to remove more humidity
Coil temperature is controlled by airflow across the coil

slides.title: Evaporator Coil Configurations
bullets: Coil made of aluminum or copper with aluminum fins
Common types: A coil, slant coil, H coil
Coils may operate in parallel and use multiple circuits with a distributor
Microchannel technology resembles automobile radiators

slides.title: The Evaporator An A Coil
bullets: Figure 36.12 An A coil

slides.title: Design Conditions
bullets: Be familiar with local design practices
Choose equipment using climate condition charts
Single-speed equipment performs most efficiently at full load
Designers may choose compromise conditions or variable capacity systems

slides.title: The Compressor
bullets: Types include reciprocating, rotary, scroll, centrifugal, and screw
Vapor pump that moves vapor from low-pressure to high-pressure side
Increases temperature and pressure of vapor

slides.title: Compressor Speeds RPM
bullets: Older compressors run at standard motor speeds of 3450 or 1750 rpm
Newer compressors operate at faster speeds with more efficient refrigerants
Most systems today use variable refrigerant flow to vary compressor speed

slides.title: The Reciprocating Compressor
bullets: Used in residential and light commercial air-conditioning
Mainly cooled by suction gas and air passing over the shell
Usually fully hermetic, suction-gas-cooled type
Positive-displacement compressors using R-22, R-410A, or R-407C

slides.title: Cooling the Compressor and Motor
bullets: Lack of cooling results in damage and breakdown of compressor oil
Hermetic compressors are suction-gas-cooled
Rotary compressors cooled with discharge gas
Technician should ensure discharge temperature is not over 225°F

slides.title: The Rotary Compressor
bullets: Small and light, sometimes cooled with discharge gas
More efficient than reciprocating compressors
Two basic designs: stationary vane and rotary vane
Discharges all refrigerant that enters it

slides.title: The Scroll Compressor
bullets: Uses two spirals, one stationary and one orbiting
Forms multiple pockets where refrigerant is trapped
Pockets get smaller as refrigerant passes through
Several compression stages occur simultaneously; highly efficient
High performance depends on good axial and radial sealing

slides.title: The Condenser
bullets: Rejects heat from the air-conditioning system
Most equipment is air-cooled and rejects heat to the air
Coils made of copper or aluminum with aluminum fins
New coil designs make units lighter and more efficient

slides.title: Side-Air-Discharge Condensing Units
bullets: Discharge air out the side of the unit
Fan and motor located under top panel
Noise generated inside cabinets discharged in airstream
Condenser discharge can be hot enough to kill plants

slides.title: Top-Air-Discharge Condensing Units
bullets: Most popular configuration in residential equipment
Hot air and noise discharge from the top
Motor on top should be protected with a rain shield
Bearings mounted vertically need thrust surface
Figure 36.30

slides.title: Condenser Coil Design
bullets: Vertical condenser coils may become clogged with grass and dirt
Coils must be clean to operate efficiently
Bottom rows may be used for subcooling to improve efficiency

slides.title: High-Efficiency Condensers
bullets: Industry and government demand higher efficiency
Best method is to lower head pressure
Physically larger coils provide greater surface area and lower head pressure

slides.title: Condenser Cabinet Design
bullets: Cabinets located outside need waterproofing
Aluminum is lightweight but susceptible to rust in salty environments
Use weather-resistant screws and fasten tightly to prevent rattling

slides.title: Expansion Devices
bullets: Control flow of refrigerant into the evaporator
Most common types are fixed-bore and TXV
Electronic expansion valves used on many ductless split systems
TXVs are more efficient and help evaporator reach peak performance faster
TXV systems may require high-starting-torque motors

slides.title: Air-Side Components
bullets: Consist of indoor blower and supply and return ducts
Airflow normally about 400 cfm/ton in average climates
350 cfm/ton in coastal areas and 450 cfm/ton in desert areas
Air leaves at 55°F and returns at about 75°F
Ductwork running through unconditioned space must be well-insulated
Supply registers located high in the room distribute cool air better

slides.title: Installation Procedures Package Systems
bullets: Package systems are self-contained (example: window unit)
Equipment located outside so service can be done outside
Factory assembled and charged with short refrigerant lines
Install on a firm foundation and connect ductwork and electrical service

slides.title: Installation Procedures Split Systems
bullets: Locate condensing unit and air handler as close together as possible
Install interconnecting piping (cool gas line and liquid line)
Cool gas line should be insulated
Take care handling equipment and watch for insects, sharp objects, weak ceilings, and refrigerant lines
